items.title: Diagnosis of Porphyrias: Laboratory and Genetic Testing
items.content: Diagnosing porphyrias involves laboratory measurement of specific biomarkers and genetic testing. Urinary levels of porphyrin precursors such as 5'-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and porphobilinogen (PBG), along with porphyrins in urine, plasma, erythrocytes, or feces, are essential for confirming the type of porphyria. A definitive diagnosis requires identifying the specific gene defect through molecular genetic analyses.
keyPoints: Laboratory measurement of ALA and PBG in urine is required to confirm or exclude types of porphyria.
Definite diagnosis necessitates demonstration of the specific gene defect.
Molecular genetic analysis allows for precise heterozygote or homozygote identification and prenatal diagnoses.

items.title: Classification and Clinical Presentation of Porphyrias
items.content: Porphyrias are classified as hepatic or erythropoietic based on the site of porphyrin precursor overproduction. Hepatic porphyrias primarily affect adults with neurologic symptoms, while erythropoietic porphyrias often present in early childhood with cutaneous photosensitivity. Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT) is an exception as a hepatic type presenting with blistering photosensitivity.
keyPoints: Porphyrias are classified as hepatic or erythropoietic based on the site of porphyrin precursor overproduction.
Hepatic porphyrias manifest primarily in adults with neurologic symptoms.
Erythropoietic porphyrias often present at birth or early childhood with cutaneous photosensitivity.

items.title: Prevalence and Genetic Patterns of Porphyrias
items.content: The porphyrias are metabolic diseases with varying prevalence across different populations, influenced by genetic factors. Acute hepatic porphyrias like AIP have a higher frequency in Caucasians of Western European descent, particularly Scandinavians, while VP is notably prevalent in South Africa due to a founder effect.
keyPoints: AIP has a frequency of ~1 in 20,000 among Caucasian individuals of Western European ancestry and ~1 in 10,000 in Sweden.
VP's high prevalence (>10,000 affected patients) in South Africa is partly due to a genetic 'founder effect'.
EPP is likely the most common porphyria with a frequency ranging from 1 in ~75,000 to 1 in ~150,000 in Caucasians.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.title: Heme Biosynthesis Pathway and Enzymatic Steps
items.content: Heme biosynthesis involves eight enzymatic steps converting glycine and succinyl-CoA to heme, with distinct mitochondrial and cytosolic enzymes. The pathway intermediates include ALA, PBG, and porphyrins, which do not accumulate significantly under normal conditions.
keyPoints: Heme biosynthesis involves eight enzymatic steps encoded by nine genes.
ALA-synthase is the rate-limiting enzyme in heme synthesis, inducible by various drugs and chemicals.
Defects in ALAS2 cause X-linked sideroblastic anemia (XLSA), while gain-of-function mutations lead to XLP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.title: Role of Enzymes in Heme Biosynthesis and Associated Disorders
items.content: Key enzymes like ALA-synthase, URO-synthase, URO-decarboxylase, COPRO-oxidase, PROTO-oxidase, and FECH play crucial roles in heme biosynthesis. Mutations or defects in these enzymes can lead to specific porphyrias.
keyPoints: ALA-synthase catalyzes the condensation of glycine and succinyl-CoA to form ALA.
URO-synthase rearranges HMB to form UROgen III, a key step in heme biosynthesis.
FECH catalyzes the final step of inserting ferrous iron into protoporphyrin IX to form heme.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.title: Regulation of ALA-synthase in Liver vs. Erythron
items.content: Heme synthesis is regulated differently in liver and erythron. In the liver, heme represses ALA-synthase 1 by affecting mRNA synthesis and enzyme translocation to mitochondria. Hepatic ALA-synthase 1 is also induced by chemicals that induce CYP enzymes, coordinating their regulation. In contrast, the erythron expresses higher levels of erythroid-specific ALA-synthase 2 during cell differentiation for hemoglobin production.
keyPoints: Heme represses hepatic ALA-synthase 1 mRNA synthesis and mitochondrial translocation.
Hepatic ALA-synthase 1 is induced by chemicals that also induce CYP enzymes, coordinating their regulation.
Erythroid-specific ALA-synthase 2 is expressed at higher levels than the housekeeping enzyme during erythropoiesis.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS

items.title: Classification and Clinical Presentation of Porphyrias
items.content: Porphyrias are classified as hepatic or erythropoietic based on the origin of heme biosynthetic intermediates, and as acute or cutaneous based on clinical manifestations. Acute hepatic porphyrias (AIP, HCP, VP) present with neurologic attacks and elevated ALA/PBG levels in adults. PCT presents with blistering skin lesions. Erythropoietic porphyrias (CEP, EPP, XLP) cause cutaneous photosensitivity due to porphyrin elevations in bone marrow and erythrocytes.
keyPoints: Acute hepatic porphyrias present with neurologic attacks and elevated ALA/PBG levels.
PCT presents with blistering skin lesions; HCP and VP may also have cutaneous manifestations.
Erythropoietic porphyrias cause photosensitivity due to elevated porphyrins in bone marrow and erythrocytes.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS

items.title: Diagnosis of Acute Hepatic Porphyria: The Role of Urinary PBG
items.content: Acute hepatic porphyrias should be suspected in patients presenting with neurovisceral symptoms post-puberty. A key diagnostic test is the measurement of urinary PBG, which is specifically increased during acute attacks of AIP, HCP, and VP, making it a sensitive and specific marker for these conditions.
keyPoints: Urinary PBG is always increased in acute attacks of AIP, HCP, and VP.
A spot urine sample measuring urinary PBG is both sensitive and specific.
24-hour urine collection is unnecessary; spot samples are sufficient.

items.title: Distinguishing Acute Porphyrias: Importance of Urinary Porphyrins
items.content: When an initial test for acute porphyria is positive, further testing with urinary porphyrins can help distinguish between AIP, HCP, and VP. This involves measuring urinary porphyrins, fecal porphyrins, and plasma porphyrins using the same spot urine sample.
keyPoints: Urinary porphyrins are measured to differentiate between AIP, HCP, and VP.
Copro-oxidase or proto-oxidase assays are not available for clinical testing.
Mutation analysis is diagnostic even when urinary ALA and PBG levels normalize.

items.title: Diagnosis of Cutaneous Porphyrias: Plasma Porphyrins and Genetic Testing
items.content: Cutaneous porphyrias are diagnosed by increased total plasma porphyrins, with a fluorometric method preferred for VP. Erythrocyte protoporphyrin determination is more sensitive than plasma porphyrin levels alone. Confirmation of diagnosis requires genetic testing to identify the causative pathogenic variant.
keyPoints: Blistering skin lesions in porphyria are accompanied by increased total plasma porphyrins.
Fluorometric method preferred for detecting VP plasma porphyrins.
Erythrocyte protoporphyrin determination is more sensitive than plasma porphyrins.

items.title: Diagnosis of ALA-Dehydratase-Deficient Porphyria (ADP)
items.content: ADP is diagnosed by identifying significantly elevated levels of plasma and urinary ALA, along with urinary coproporphyrin III. Erythrocyte ALA-dehydratase activity should be <10% of normal in affected individuals. Differential diagnoses include hereditary tyrosinemia type 1 and lead intoxication due to similar biochemical profiles.
keyPoints: Elevated plasma and urinary ALA levels
Urinary coproporphyrin III elevation
<10% normal erythrocyte ALA-dehydratase activity in affected individuals
Differential diagnosis includes hereditary tyrosinemia type 1 and lead intoxication
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.title: Treatment of Acute Attacks in ADP
items.content: Acute attacks in ADP are managed similarly to those in AIP, involving supportive care. In severe cases, such as the severely affected infant, treatment may include hyperalimentation and periodic blood transfusions. Intravenous hemin is not effective, and liver transplantation may be considered.
keyPoints: Management similar to AIP
Supportive care with hyperalimentation and blood transfusions in severe cases
Intravenous hemin ineffective
Liver transplantation as a potential treatment option
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.title: Understanding the Triggers of Acute Attacks in AIP
items.content: Acute attacks in Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) are often precipitated by factors that increase hepatic heme synthesis, leading to accumulation and urinary excretion of porphyrin precursors. These triggers include endogenous and exogenous steroids, certain drugs, alcohol ingestion, and low-calorie diets.
keyPoints: Half-normal HMB-synthase activity can become limiting under increased heme synthesis conditions.
Common precipitating factors: steroids (endogenous/exogenous), porphyrinogenic drugs, alcohol, low-calorie diets.
Pregnancy is usually well tolerated despite high progesterone levels.

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of AIP
items.content: AIP often remains latent until puberty, with symptoms more common in women. Neurovisceral symptoms include abdominal pain, tachycardia, hypertension, and peripheral neuropathy due to axonal degeneration. High suspicion is needed for diagnosis as symptoms are nonspecific.
keyPoints: Symptoms usually manifest post-puberty; more common in women.
Neurovisceral symptoms: abdominal pain, tachycardia, hypertension, sympathetic overactivity.
Peripheral neuropathy affects motor neurons, leading to proximal muscle weakness.

items.title: Management and Complications of AIP Attacks
items.content: AIP attacks can lead to severe complications like respiratory paralysis if untreated. Mental symptoms, seizures, and hyponatremia are possible during attacks. Treatment involves avoiding triggers and managing symptoms; clonazepam may be safer for seizures than other antiseizure drugs.
keyPoints: Untreated AIP can progress to respiratory/bulbar paralysis and death.
Mental symptoms include anxiety, hallucinations; seizures are challenging to treat.
Hyponatremia results from inappropriate vasopressin secretion or electrolyte depletion.

items.title: Understanding Homozygous Dominant AIP and Its Clinical Presentation
items.content: Homozygous dominant Acute Intermittent Porphyria (HD-AIP) is a rare form where patients inherit HMBS mutations from both parents, resulting in very low enzyme activity (<2%). It presents early with failure to thrive, developmental delay, bilateral cataracts, and/or hepatosplenomegaly. Brain MRIs indicate damage primarily in postnatally myelinated white matter.
keyPoints: Very low HMBS enzyme activity (<2%)
Clinical presentation includes failure to thrive, developmental delay, bilateral cataracts, hepatosplenomegaly
Brain MRI shows damage in postnatal white matter
Most children with HD-AIP die at an early age

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Management of Acute Attacks in AIP
items.content: Diagnosis of acute attacks in AIP relies on clinical features, as urinary ALA and PBG levels remain elevated post-symptom resolution. Urinary PBG excretion during an attack is typically 50–200 mg/24 h (220–880 μmol/24 h), and ALA excretion is 20–100 mg/24 h (150–760 μmol/24 h). A normal urinary PBG level before hemin excludes AIP as the cause of symptoms.
keyPoints: Urinary PBG during attack: 50–200 mg/24 h (220–880 μmol/24 h)
Urinary ALA during attack: 20–100 mg/24 h (150–760 μmol/24 h)
Normal urinary PBG before hemin excludes AIP
Diagnosis based on clinical features when levels remain high post-symptom resolution

items.title: First-Line Therapy for Acute Attacks of AIP
items.content: Intravenous hemin is a first-line therapy for acute attacks of acute intermittent porphyria (AIP). It is administered at 3–4 mg/kg per day, infused daily for 4 days or longer to reduce pain. Heme arginate and heme albumin are preferred over hematin due to their stability and lower risk of phlebitis.
keyPoints: Intravenous hemin approved by FDA in 1984
Standard regimen: 3–4 mg/kg per day for 4 days or longer
Heme arginate and heme albumin preferred due to stability

items.title: Role of Givosiran in AIP Management
items.content: Givosiran, a monthly subcutaneous injection of 2.5 mg/kg, is an RNA interference therapy approved for acute hepatic porphyrias. It reduces ALA and PBG levels, decreases the frequency of acute attacks, lowers hemin use, and improves pain scores.
keyPoints: Approved by FDA and EMA in 2019
Monthly subcutaneous injection: 2.5 mg/kg
Reduces ALA and PBG levels, attack frequency, hemin use

items.title: Long-term Complications and Management in AIP
items.content: Patients with AIP have an increased risk of hypertension, chronic renal disease (59% develop CKD), and hepatocellular carcinoma. Orthotopic liver transplantation is effective for severe cases but should be a last resort due to associated risks.
keyPoints: Increased risk of hypertension and CKD in AIP
Up to 59% develop chronic kidney disease
OLT improves symptoms but has significant morbidity/mortality

items.title: Understanding the Enzymatic Deficiency and Genetic Basis of PCT
items.content: PCT is characterized by a deficiency in hepatic URO-decarboxylase, which must be substantial (~20% or less) for clinical symptoms to manifest. The majority of patients (~80%) have no UROD mutations (sporadic type 1), while familial type 2 results from heterozygous UROD mutations leading to ~50% enzymatic activity.
keyPoints: Hepatic URO-decarboxylase deficiency must be substantial (~20% or less) for clinical symptoms of PCT.
~80% of PCT patients have no UROD mutations (sporadic type 1).
Familial type 2 PCT results from heterozygous UROD mutations, leading to ~50% enzymatic activity.

items.title: Identifying Clinical Features and Susceptibility Factors of PCT
items.content: PCT presents with blistering skin lesions on sun-exposed areas, hypertrichosis, and hyperpigmentation. Key susceptibility factors include hepatitis C, HIV, excess alcohol, elevated iron levels, estrogens, and certain genetic mutations like HFE p.C282Y and p.H63D.
keyPoints: Blistering skin lesions on sun-exposed areas are a major clinical feature of PCT.
Susceptibility factors include hepatitis C, HIV, excess alcohol, elevated iron levels, estrogens, and HFE mutations (p.C282Y and p.H63D).

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for PCT
items.content: Diagnosis of PCT involves increased porphyrins in liver, plasma, urine, and stool. Urinary ALA may be slightly elevated, but PBG is normal. Type 2 PCT and HEP can be distinguished by decreased URO-decarboxylase activity in erythrocytes (~50% in type 2, 3–10% in HEP).
keyPoints: Increased porphyrins in liver, plasma, urine, and stool are diagnostic for PCT.
Urinary ALA may be slightly increased; PBG is normal.
Type 2 PCT shows ~50% URO-decarboxylase activity in erythrocytes; HEP shows 3–10%.

items.title: Phlebotomy as Standard Therapy for Porphyria Cutanea Tarda (PCT)
items.content: Phlebotomy is the standard treatment for PCT, aiming to reduce hepatic iron levels. The procedure involves removing 450 mL of blood every 1–2 weeks until serum ferritin reaches normal limits. Most patients achieve remission after five or six phlebotomies unless they have hemochromatosis, which may require more treatments.
keyPoints: Phlebotomy reduces hepatic iron and is the standard therapy for PCT.
A unit (450 mL) of blood can be removed every 1–2 weeks.
Remission typically occurs after five or six phlebotomies, unless hemochromatosis is present.
Monitor serum ferritin to prevent iron deficiency and anemia.

items.title: Alternative Treatments for PCT When Phlebotomy is Contraindicated
items.content: When phlebotomy is not suitable, low-dose chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine can be used to treat PCT. These medications complex with excess porphyrins and promote their excretion. Low doses (e.g., 125 mg of chloroquine phosphate twice weekly) are recommended to avoid increased photosensitivity and hepatocellular damage.
keyPoints: Low-dose chloroquine or hydroxychloroquine is an alternative when phlebotomy is contraindicated.
Doses should be small (e.g., 125 mg of chloroquine phosphate twice weekly) to prevent side effects.
Hydroxychloroquine is as safe and effective as phlebotomy in PCT.

items.title: Diagnosis of Hereditary Coproporphyria (HCP)
items.content: Hereditary Coproporphyria is diagnosed by identifying increased fecal porphyrins, primarily consisting of copro III, which distinguishes it from other porphyrias. Urinary ALA and PBG levels are elevated during acute attacks but normalize more quickly than in AIP.
keyPoints: COPRO III markedly increased in urine and feces in symptomatic patients; persists especially in feces when asymptomatic.
Urinary ALA and PBG levels are increased (less than in AIP) during acute attacks, normalizing more quickly post-attack.
Plasma porphyrins usually normal or slightly increased but may be higher with skin lesions.
Diagnosis confirmed by increased fecal porphyrins consisting almost entirely of COPRO III.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ☑ HEREDITARY COPROPORPHYRIA | Hereditary Coproporphyria

items.title: Genetic and Clinical Management of Hereditary Coproporphyria
items.content: HCP is an autosomal dominant disorder with over 95 mutations identified in the CPOX gene. Neurologic symptoms are managed similarly to AIP, but phlebotomy and chloroquine are ineffective for cutaneous lesions.
keyPoints: Autosomal dominant disorder influenced by factors causing attacks in AIP.
>95 mutations identified in the CPOX gene; ~70% are missense or nonsense.
Neurologic symptoms treated as in AIP.
Phlebotomy and chloroquine ineffective for cutaneous lesions.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ☑ HEREDITARY COPROPORPHYRIA | Hereditary Coproporphyria

items.title: Diagnosis of Variegate Porphyria (VP) via Plasma Porphyrin Fluorescence
items.content: Variegate porphyria can be rapidly distinguished from other porphyrias by examining the fluorescence emission spectrum of plasma porphyrins, which shows a unique peak at neutral pH.
keyPoints: Unique fluorescence peak at neutral pH in VP distinguishes it from other porphyrias
Plasma porphyrin levels are increased, particularly with cutaneous lesions

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Prevalence of Variegate Porphyria (VP)
items.content: Variegate porphyria presents with skin photosensitivity, acute neurovisceral crises, or both. Approximately 60% have only skin lesions, 20% have only acute attacks, and ~20% experience both. VP is notably prevalent in South Africa, affecting about 3 out of every 1000 whites.
keyPoints: Approximately 60% of VP patients present with only skin lesions
About 20% present with only acute neurovisceral attacks
Around 20% experience both symptoms
Prevalence in South Africa is about 3 per 1000 whites

items.title: Management of Acute Attacks in Variegate Porphyria
items.content: Acute attacks in variegate porphyria are managed similarly to acute intermittent porphyria (AIP). Hemin is recommended early for most cases, and givosiran has shown efficacy in clinical trials for patients with recurrent attacks.
keyPoints: Hemin should be started early in most cases of acute attacks.
Givosiran is effective in clinical trials for recurrent attacks.
Avoiding sun exposure is advised; however, $ \beta $-carotene, phlebotomy, and chloroquine are not helpful.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of X-Linked Sideroblastic Anemia (XLSA)
items.content: XLSA is characterized by deficient activity of erythroid ALA-synthase 2, leading to ineffective erythropoiesis. Diagnosis involves bone marrow examination showing hypercellularity with megaloblastic erythropoiesis and sideroblasts. Definitive diagnosis requires identifying loss-of-function mutations in the erythroid ALAS2 gene. Treatment may include pyridoxine supplementation for responsive patients, while unresponsive cases might need transfusions and chelation therapy.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves bone marrow examination revealing hypercellularity with megaloblastic erythropoiesis and sideroblasts.
Definitive diagnosis requires identification of loss-of-function mutations in the erythroid ALAS2 gene (>120 identified).
Treatment includes pyridoxine supplementation for responsive patients; unresponsive cases may require transfusions and chelation therapy.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA

items.title: Diagnosis of Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria (CEP)
items.content: CEP, an autosomal recessive disorder due to deficient URO-synthase activity, can be diagnosed by detecting elevated levels of specific porphyrins and confirming markedly deficient URO-synthase activity. Molecular analyses identifying mutations in the UROS gene are crucial for diagnosis.
keyPoints: CEP is associated with accumulation of URO I and COPRO I isomers due to deficient URO-synthase activity.
Diagnosis can be confirmed by demonstrating markedly deficient URO-synthase activity or specific UROS gene mutations.
The predominant porphyrin in feces is COPRO I.
>65 mutations have been identified in the UROS gene, which can help predict disease severity.

items.title: Clinical Features and Complications of Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria (CEP)
items.content: CEP presents with severe cutaneous photosensitivity from birth, leading to skin fragility, bullae, vesicles, and secondary infections. Porphyrin deposition causes brownish teeth that fluoresce under long-wave UV light. Hemolysis due to increased erythrocyte porphyrins results in splenomegaly.
keyPoints: Severe cutaneous photosensitivity begins from birth with skin over exposed areas being friable and prone to infection.
Characteristic features include skin thickening, focal hypo- and hyperpigmentation, and hypertrichosis of the face and extremities.
Porphyrins deposited in teeth cause them to be brownish and fluoresce under long-wave UV light.
Hemolysis leads to splenomegaly due to increased erythrocyte porphyrins.

items.title: Management of Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria
items.content: Transfusion-dependent patients require periodic transfusions to manage anemia, but this can lead to iron overload. Oral iron chelation is recommended to mitigate this risk. Splenectomy may be beneficial in reducing hemolysis and decreasing transfusion needs.
keyPoints: Periodic transfusions are needed for anemia management in transfusion-dependent patients.
Iron overload from chronic transfusions necessitates oral iron chelation.
Splenectomy can reduce hemolysis and decrease transfusion requirements.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis and Genetic Basis of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria
items.content: EPP is an autosomal recessive disorder due to deficient FECH activity. Most symptomatic patients inherit a deleterious mutation in one FECH allele along with the common IVS3–48T>C variant on the other allele, resulting in low enzyme expression.
keyPoints: EPP results from deficient FECH activity, an autosomal recessive disorder.
FECH activities in EPP patients are 15-30% of normal.
>95% of symptomatic patients have a deleterious mutation with the IVS3–48T>C variant.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Features and Complications of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria
items.content: EPP patients experience skin photosensitivity starting in early childhood, with prodromal symptoms as a warning signal. Hepatic complications can occur in ~2-5% of patients, potentially progressing to liver failure.
keyPoints: Photosensitivity begins in early childhood and is characterized by tingling or burning sensations within 10-30 minutes of sun exposure.
>60% of patients experience prodromal symptoms within an hour of sun exposure.
~2-5% of EPP patients may develop chronic liver disease leading to liver failure.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis of EPP: Importance of Distinguishing Free vs. Zinc-Complexed Protoporphyrin IX
items.content: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) is characterized by a significant increase in erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX, predominantly free and not complexed with zinc. This distinguishes EPP from other conditions like lead poisoning or iron deficiency where protoporphyrin IX is zinc-complexed. Confirming the diagnosis requires assays that differentiate between free and zinc-complexed protoporphyrin.
keyPoints: Erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX increase in EPP is predominantly free, not complexed with zinc.
Other conditions with increased erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX have it complexed with zinc.
Diagnosis confirmation requires assays distinguishing free from zinc-complexed protoporphyrin.
Erythrocytes exhibit red fluorescence under 620 nm in EPP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 2) | Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

items.title: Differential Diagnosis: XLP vs. EPP
items.content: X-linked protoporphyria (XLP) can be differentiated from EPP by higher erythrocyte protoporphyrin levels and a significant proportion of free protoporphyrin IX, which may reach 50%. XLP accounts for approximately 2% of patients with the EPP phenotype in Western Europe and about 10% in North America.
keyPoints: XLP has higher erythrocyte protoporphyrin levels than EPP.
In XLP, free and zinc protoporphyrin IX proportions may reach 50%.
~2% of patients with the EPP phenotype in Western Europe have XLP.
~10% of North American patients with the EPP phenotype have XLP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 2) | Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

items.question: Which of the following best describes the inheritance pattern of porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT)?
items.explanation: Porphyria cutanea tarda is usually sporadic, unlike other porphyrias which are inherited.

items.question: Which type of porphyria primarily presents with neurologic symptoms such as neuropathic abdominal pain and peripheral motor neuropathy?
items.explanation: Acute hepatic porphyrias primarily present with neurologic symptoms.

items.question: What laboratory measurements are required to confirm or exclude the various types of porphyria?
options: Measurement of heme levels in plasma
Urinary measurement of 5'-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and porphobilinogen (PBG)
items.explanation: Laboratory diagnosis involves measuring porphyrin precursors like ALA and PBG in the urine.

items.question: Which enzyme catalyzes the condensation of glycine and succinyl-CoA to form ALA in heme biosynthesis?
items.explanation: ALA-synthase catalyzes the condensation of glycine and succinyl-CoA to form ALA.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.question: Which enzyme is responsible for converting protoporphyrinogen IX to protoporphyrin IX?
items.explanation: PROTO-oxidase catalyzes the oxidation of protoporphyrinogen IX to protoporphyrin IX.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.question: What is the role of ferrous iron in heme biosynthesis?
options: It catalyzes the formation of ALA
It converts protoporphyrinogen IX to protoporphyrin IX
It inserts into protoporphyrin IX to form heme
It removes carboxyl groups from uroporphyrinogen III
items.explanation: Ferrous iron is inserted into protoporphyrin IX by FECH to form heme.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.question: Which gene encodes the erythroid-specific form of ALA-synthase?
items.explanation: The gene ALAS2 encodes the erythroid-specific form of ALA-synthase.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS
items.question: Which enzyme's synthesis is repressed by heme in the liver?
items.explanation: In the liver, heme represses the synthesis of ALA-synthase 1 mRNA.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of erythroid-specific regulation of heme biosynthesis?
options: Expression of separate transcripts for the first four enzymes in the pathway
Constant expression of all heme biosynthetic enzymes
items.explanation: Erythroid-specific regulation involves separate erythroid-specific and nonerythroid transcripts for the first four enzymes in the pathway.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS
items.question: Which hepatic porphyria is characterized by blistering skin lesions?
items.explanation: PCT (Porphyria Cutanea Tarda) presents with blistering skin lesions.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS

items.question: Which urinary porphyrin precursor is always increased during acute attacks of AIP, HCP, and VP?
items.explanation: Urinary PBG is always increased during acute attacks of AIP, HCP, and VP.

items.question: Why should measurement of urinary porphyrins alone be avoided for screening porphyria?
options: They are not increased in any medical condition.
They may be increased in disorders other than porphyrias, such as chronic liver disease.
They are always decreased during acute attacks.
They provide a definitive diagnosis of porphyria.
items.explanation: Urinary porphyrins may be increased in disorders other than porphyrias, leading to potential misdiagnosis.

items.question: What is the preferred method for detecting plasma porphyrins in VP?
items.explanation: A fluorometric method is preferred for detecting plasma porphyrins in VP.

items.question: Which test confirms the diagnosis of EPP by showing a predominant increase?
options: Measurement of urinary ALA and PBG
items.explanation: The diagnosis of EPP is confirmed by showing a predominant increase in free protoporphyrin.

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency causes Ala-dehydratase-deficient porphyria (ADP)?
items.explanation: ADP is caused by a severe deficiency of ALA-dehydratase activity.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.question: What clinical feature is common in patients with ADP who have more significant enzyme deficiency?
options: Failure to thrive beginning at birth
items.explanation: An infant with a more significant deficiency had failure to thrive beginning at birth.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.question: What is the typical erythrocyte ALA-dehydratase activity level in patients with ADP?
items.explanation: Affected homozygotes had <10% of normal ALA-dehydratase activity in erythrocytes.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.question: Which enzyme activity is half-normal in individuals with Acute Intermittent Porphyria?
items.explanation: Acute Intermittent Porphyria is characterized by half-normal HMB-synthase activity.

items.question: What factor is suggested to be important for the clinical expression of Acute Intermittent Porphyria?
items.explanation: The fact that AIP is almost always latent before puberty suggests that adult levels of steroid hormones are important for clinical expression.

items.question: Which type of neuropathy primarily affects motor neurons in Acute Intermittent Porphyria?
items.explanation: The peripheral neuropathy in AIP is due to axonal degeneration affecting motor neurons.

items.question: Which of the following can exacerbate Acute Intermittent Porphyria attacks?
items.explanation: Exogenous steroids, including oral contraceptives with progestins, can exacerbate AIP attacks.

items.question: Which of the following urinary excretion levels are typically observed during an acute attack of AIP?
options: Urinary PBG: 0–4 mg/24 h, Urinary ALA: 1–7 mg/24 h
Urinary PBG: 50–200 mg/24 h, Urinary ALA: 20–100 mg/24 h
Urinary PBG: 220–880 μmol/24 h, Urinary ALA: 8–53 μmol/24 h
Urinary PBG and ALA levels remain normal during an acute attack
items.explanation: During an acute attack of AIP, urinary PBG excretion is usually 50–200 mg/24 h and urinary ALA excretion is 20–100 mg/24 h.

items.question: In patients with HMBS mutations affecting the initiation of translation codon in exon 1, what is observed regarding enzyme levels?
options: Normal enzyme levels in both erythrocytes and nonerythroid tissues
Deficient activity only in nonerythroid tissues
No enzyme activity detected in any tissue
items.explanation: Patients with HMBS mutations affecting the initiation of translation codon in exon 1 have normal enzyme levels in erythrocytes and deficient activity only in nonerythroid tissues.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of the urinary porphyrin profile in AIP compared to HCP and VP?
options: Urinary PBG levels remain high after symptoms resolve
Fecal porphyrins are usually normal or minimally increased
High urinary excretion of ALA and PBG without clinical symptoms
items.explanation: In AIP, fecal porphyrins are usually normal or minimally increased, unlike in HCP and VP.

items.question: Which medication is used as a first-line therapy to treat acute attacks of Acute Intermittent Porphyria?
items.explanation: Intravenous hemin is used as a first-line therapy for acute attacks of Acute Intermittent Porphyria.

items.question: What is the purpose of givosiran in the treatment of Acute Intermittent Porphyria?
options: To reduce ALA and PBG levels
items.explanation: Givosiran is designed to silence the expression of hepatic ALAS1 mRNA, reducing ALA and PBG levels in patients.

items.question: What long-term risk is increased in Acute Intermittent Porphyria?
items.explanation: The long-term risk of hypertension and chronic renal disease is increased in patients with Acute Intermittent Porphyria.

items.question: Which genotype affects the severity and prognosis of porphyria-associated kidney disease?
items.explanation: The PEPT2 $^{*1}$ allele and related genotypes affect the severity and prognosis of porphyria-associated kidney disease.

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency is a common feature of all types of Porphyria Cutanea Tarda (PCT)?
items.explanation: Hepatic URO-decarboxylase deficiency is a common feature in all types of PCT.

items.question: What percentage of normal enzymatic activity is typically seen in erythrocytes of individuals with type 2 PCT?
items.explanation: In type 2 PCT, URO-decarboxylase activity in erythrocytes is ~50% of normal.

items.question: Which condition can be distinguished from type 1 PCT by decreased URO-decarboxylase activity in erythrocytes?
items.explanation: Type 2 PCT and HEP can be distinguished from type 1 by decreased URO-decarboxylase activity in erythrocytes.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognized susceptibility factor for Porphyria Cutanea Tarda?
items.explanation: Hyperthyroidism is not listed as a susceptibility factor for PCT.

items.question: What is the standard therapy to achieve complete response in Porphyria Cutanea Tarda (PCT)?
options: Repeated phlebotomy to reduce hepatic iron
items.explanation: Standard therapy involves repeated phlebotomy to reduce hepatic iron.

items.question: Which of the following is a potential complication that can be diagnosed using hepatic imaging in PCT patients?
items.explanation: Hepatic imaging can diagnose or exclude complicating hepatocellular carcinoma.

items.question: What should be monitored closely to prevent iron deficiency and anemia during PCT treatment?
options: Hemoglobin levels or hematocrits and serum ferritin
items.explanation: Hemoglobin levels, hematocrits, and serum ferritin should be monitored to prevent iron deficiency and anemia.

items.question: Which enzyme activity is reduced in hereditary coproporphyria (HCP)?
items.explanation: Hereditary coproporphyria results from half-normal activity of COPRO-oxidase.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ☑ HEREDITARY COPROPORPHYRIA | Hereditary Coproporphyria

items.question: Which porphyrin is markedly increased in the urine and feces of symptomatic HCP patients?
items.explanation: COPRO III is markedly increased in the urine and feces of symptomatic HCP patients.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ☑ HEREDITARY COPROPORPHYRIA | Hereditary Coproporphyria

items.question: Which treatment is NOT effective for cutaneous lesions in hereditary coproporphyria?
options: Neurologic symptom management as in AIP
items.explanation: Phlebotomy and chloroquine are not effective for the cutaneous lesions in HCP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ☑ HEREDITARY COPROPORPHYRIA | Hereditary Coproporphyria

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency is responsible for variegate porphyria (VP)?
items.explanation: Variegate porphyria results from deficient activity of PROTO-oxidase.

items.question: What is a characteristic finding in homozygous variegate porphyria patients?
options: Decreased erythrocyte levels of zinc protoporphyrin
Increased urinary ALA and PBG levels during acute attacks
Unique fluorescence peak at neutral pH in plasma porphyrins
Increased erythrocyte levels of zinc protoporphyrin
items.explanation: Homozygous variegate porphyria patients have increased erythrocyte levels of zinc protoporphyrin.

items.question: How can VP be rapidly distinguished from other porphyrias?
options: By examining the fluorescence emission spectrum of porphyrins in plasma
By measuring urinary ALA and PBG levels
By identifying mutations in the PPOX gene
items.explanation: VP has a unique fluorescence peak at neutral pH, allowing rapid distinction from other porphyrias.

items.subtopic: Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA
items.question: Which of the following treatments is NOT effective for skin lesions in variegate porphyria?
items.explanation: $\beta$-Carotene is not helpful for treating skin lesions in variegate porphyria.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA

items.subtopic: Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA
items.question: What is the primary mechanism leading to cutaneous photosensitivity in erythropoietic porphyrias?
options: Excess porphyrins from bone marrow are transported via plasma to the skin.
Increased urinary and fecal porphyrin levels.
items.explanation: Excess porphyrins from bone marrow erythrocyte precursors are transported via the plasma to the skin in erythropoietic porphyrias.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA

items.subtopic: Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA
items.question: Which of the following is a diagnostic feature observed in bone marrow examination for X-linked sideroblastic anemia (XLSA)?
options: Normal levels of urinary porphyrin precursors.
items.explanation: Bone marrow examination in XLSA reveals megaloblastic erythropoiesis with an abnormal maturation.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA

items.question: What enzyme deficiency is primarily responsible for Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria (CEP)?
items.explanation: CEP is due to markedly deficient activity of URO-synthase.

items.question: Which porphyrin is predominantly found in the feces of patients with Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria?
items.explanation: The predominant porphyrin in feces of CEP patients is COPRO I.

items.question: Which clinical feature is NOT typically associated with Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria?
options: Hypertrichosis of the face and extremities
items.explanation: CEP is not typically associated with neurological impairment.

items.question: What is the primary treatment approach for transfusion-dependent patients with Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria to manage iron overload?
items.explanation: Oral iron chelation is recommended for managing iron overload in transfusion-dependent patients.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.question: What surgical procedure may reduce hemolysis and decrease transfusion requirements in Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria?
items.explanation: Splenectomy may reduce hemolysis and decrease transfusion requirements in Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.question: What is the genetic inheritance pattern of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria?
items.explanation: Erythropoietic Protoporphyria is an autosomal recessive disorder.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme's deficient activity causes Erythropoietic Protoporphyria?
items.explanation: Erythropoietic Protoporphyria results from deficient activity of ferrochelatase (FECH).
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.question: Which assay is crucial to confirm the diagnosis of erythropoietic protoporphyria after finding increased erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX?
options: An assay distinguishing free and zinc-complexed protoporphyrin.
A fluorescence microscopy test at 620 nm.
Measurement of urinary porphyrins and precursors.
Assessment of FECH activity in cultured lymphocytes.
items.explanation: Distinguishing free from zinc-complexed protoporphyrin confirms EPP diagnosis.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 2) | Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

items.question: Which of the following conditions is characterized by erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX that is complexed with zinc?
options: All homozygous forms of other porphyrias.
items.explanation: In lead poisoning, erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX is complexed with zinc.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 2) | Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

items.question: What percentage of North American patients with the EPP phenotype have X-linked protoporphyria?
items.explanation: ~10% of North American patients with the EPP phenotype have XLP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 2) | Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

items.statement: Erythropoietic porphyrias typically present with cutaneous photosensitivity.
items.explanation: Erythropoietic porphyrias often manifest as cutaneous photosensitivity due to excess porphyrins in the skin.

items.statement: A definitive diagnosis of porphyria can be made solely based on clinical symptoms.
items.explanation: Diagnosis requires laboratory measurement and identification of specific gene defects, not just clinical symptoms.

items.statement: Heme biosynthesis involves nine genes encoding eight enzymes.
items.explanation: The pathway has eight enzymatic steps encoded by nine genes due to two genes for ALA-synthase.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.statement: Heme is primarily synthesized in the mitochondria.
items.explanation: Most heme synthesis occurs in the cytosol, with only some steps occurring in the mitochondria.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS
items.statement: Hepatic ALA-synthase 1 and CYP enzymes are regulated independently.
items.explanation: Hepatic ALA-synthase 1 and the CYPs are regulated in a coordinated fashion.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS
items.statement: EPP can lead to liver failure requiring transplantation.
items.explanation: About 5% of EPP patients develop hepatic complications that can lead to liver failure requiring transplantation.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · REGULATION OF HEME BIOSYNTHESIS | CLASSIFICATION OF THE PORPHYRIAS

items.statement: A 24-hour urine collection is necessary for diagnosing acute porphyrias.
items.explanation: Results from spot (single-void) urine specimens are highly informative, making a 24-h collection unnecessary.

items.statement: Measurement of erythrocyte HMB-synthase is useful as a first-line test for diagnosing acute porphyrias.
items.explanation: Measurement of erythrocyte HMB-synthase is not useful as a first-line test.

items.statement: Heterozygotes for ADP excrete increased levels of ALA.
items.explanation: Heterozygous individuals do not excrete increased levels of ALA.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.statement: Prenatal diagnosis of ADP is possible by determining gene mutations.
items.explanation: Prenatal diagnosis can be done by determination of ALA-dehydratase activity and/or gene mutations.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.statement: Pregnancy is usually poorly tolerated in patients with Acute Intermittent Porphyria.
items.explanation: Surprisingly, pregnancy is usually well tolerated in AIP.

items.statement: The peripheral neuropathy in Acute Intermittent Porphyria primarily affects sensory neurons.
items.explanation: The peripheral neuropathy in AIP is due to axonal degeneration affecting motor neurons.

items.statement: Most children with homozygous AIP survive into adulthood.
items.explanation: Most children with homozygous AIP die at an early age.

items.statement: A normal urinary PBG level before hemin administration effectively excludes AIP as a cause for current symptoms.
items.explanation: A normal urinary PBG level before hemin effectively excludes AIP as a cause for current symptoms.

items.statement: Orthotopic liver transplantation is recommended as a first-line treatment for Acute Intermittent Porphyria.
items.explanation: Orthotopic liver transplantation should be considered a last resort due to associated morbidity and mortality.

items.statement: Givosiran is administered as a monthly subcutaneous injection.
items.explanation: Givosiran is given as a 2.5 mg/kg monthly subcutaneous injection.

items.statement: Neurologic features are commonly present in patients with Porphyria Cutanea Tarda.
items.explanation: Neurologic features are absent in PCT.

items.statement: Exposure to hexachlorobenzene can induce Porphyria Cutanea Tarda.
items.explanation: An epidemic of PCT occurred in eastern Turkey due to wheat contaminated with hexachlorobenzene.

items.statement: Phlebotomy is always required after remission in PCT.
items.explanation: After remission, continued phlebotomy may not be needed.

items.statement: Low-dose hydroxychloroquine is as safe and effective as phlebotomy in PCT.
items.explanation: Studies indicate that low-dose hydroxychloroquine is as safe and effective as phlebotomy.

items.statement: Hereditary coproporphyria is more common than acute intermittent porphyria (AIP).
items.explanation: HCP is less common than AIP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ☑ HEREDITARY COPROPORPHYRIA | Hereditary Coproporphyria

items.statement: Diagnosis of HCP can be confirmed by increased fecal porphyrins consisting almost entirely of COPRO III.
items.explanation: Increased fecal porphyrins consisting almost entirely of COPRO III distinguish HCP from other porphyrias.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ☑ HEREDITARY COPROPORPHYRIA | Hereditary Coproporphyria

items.statement: Acute attacks in VP are precipitated by different factors than those in AIP.
items.explanation: Acute attacks in VP are identical to those in AIP and are precipitated by the same factors.

items.statement: The missense mutation encoding p.R59W is common among English and French patients with VP.
items.explanation: The p.R59W mutation is the common mutation in most South Africans of Dutch descent, not English or French.

items.subtopic: Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA
items.statement: Levels of urinary and fecal porphyrins are elevated in X-linked sideroblastic anemia.
items.explanation: Urinary and fecal porphyrin levels are normal in X-linked sideroblastic anemia.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA

items.subtopic: Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA
items.statement: Pyridoxine supplementation can be effective in treating severe anemia associated with X-linked sideroblastic anemia.
items.explanation: Severe anemia in XLSA may respond to pyridoxine supplementation.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Variegate Porphyria | THE ERYTHROPOIETIC PORPHYRIAS | X-LINKED SIDEROBLASTIC ANEMIA

items.statement: Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria can be diagnosed by measuring porphyrins in amniotic fluid.
items.explanation: CEP can be detected in utero by measuring porphyrins in amniotic fluid.

items.statement: The teeth of patients with Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria are typically unaffected.
items.explanation: Porphyrins are deposited in the teeth, causing them to be brownish and fluoresce under ultraviolet light.

items.statement: In Erythropoietic Protoporphyria, protoporphyrin IX is mostly bound to zinc in erythrocytes.
items.explanation: In EPP patients, erythrocyte protoporphyrin IX is free (not complexed with zinc).
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.statement: Hepatic complications in EPP are often associated with increasing levels of protoporphyrins.
items.explanation: Hepatic complications in EPP are characterized by increasing levels of protoporphyrins.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.statement: Urinary levels of porphyrins and porphyrin precursors are increased in erythropoietic protoporphyria.
items.explanation: Urinary levels of porphyrins and porphyrin precursors are normal in EPP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 2) | Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

items.statement: FECH activity is decreased in cultured lymphocytes or fibroblasts from patients with erythropoietic protoporphyria.
items.explanation: FECH activity is indeed decreased (<30% of normal mean) in EPP.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 2) | Erythropoietic Protoporphyria

items.assertion: Environmental factors can precipitate attacks in acute hepatic porphyrias.
items.reason: Attacks are often triggered by dieting, certain drugs, and hormonal changes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why environmental factors can trigger attacks.

items.assertion: The frequency of EPP varies globally.
items.reason: Most patients have a common low expression FECH allele, which has different frequencies in various populations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the variation in EPP frequency.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.assertion: ALA-synthase is not involved in heme biosynthesis.
items.reason: ALA-synthase catalyzes the condensation of glycine and succinyl-CoA to form ALA, initiating heme synthesis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ALA-synthase plays a crucial role in heme biosynthesis.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | HEME BIOSYNTHESIS

items.assertion: Acute hepatic porphyrias can be distinguished by measuring urinary porphyrins.
items.reason: Urinary porphyrins are specific for acute hepatic porphyrias and not increased in other conditions.
items.explanation: The reason is false because urinary porphyrin increases are often nonspecific.

items.assertion: Genetic testing can facilitate diagnosis and genetic counseling of at-risk relatives.
items.reason: Detection of the specific gene mutation in the index case confirms porphyria even when biochemical markers are normal.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: ADP can be differentiated from hereditary tyrosinemia type 1 and lead intoxication.
items.reason: Both conditions inhibit ALA-dehydratase, increasing urinary excretion of ALA and COPRO III.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains the assertion as both conditions can mimic acute porphyrias by inhibiting ALA-dehydratase.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · THE HEPATIC PORPHYRIAS | ALA-DEHYDRATASE-DEFICIENT PORPHYRIA

items.assertion: Acute attacks of Acute Intermittent Porphyria are often related to environmental or hormonal factors.
items.reason: Activation of the disease is often related to drugs, diet, and steroid hormones.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A.

items.assertion: Abdominal pain in Acute Intermittent Porphyria is usually associated with fever and leukocytosis.
items.reason: The symptoms are neurologic rather than inflammatory, so fever and leukocytosis are usually absent or mild.
items.explanation: A is false because abdominal pain is not usually associated with fever and leukocytosis; R is true.

items.assertion: Hemin therapy can reduce the frequency of acute attacks in Acute Intermittent Porphyria.
items.reason: Hemin is used as a first-line therapy to treat acute attacks and reduces pain.
items.explanation: While hemin is effective for treating acute attacks, it does not reduce the frequency of future attacks.

items.assertion: The PEPT2 receptor polymorphic genotype influences kidney function in porphyria.
items.reason: The high affinity PEPT2 $^{*1}$ allele is associated with decreasing kidney function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Hepatolenticular degeneration is a form of Porphyria Cutanea Tarda.
items.reason: HEP is an autosomal recessive form of porphyria due to two pathogenic UROD mutations.
items.explanation: Hepatolenticular degeneration is not related to PCT; HEP is a separate condition.

items.assertion: Patients with type 2 PCT have half-normal URO-decarboxylase activity in liver and other tissues.
items.reason: Inheritance of one UROD mutation results in significant predisposition but not enough to cause symptoms alone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Bone marrow and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation has been curative in transfusion-dependent children with Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria.
items.reason: This provides the rationale for future stem cell gene therapy.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why bone marrow transplantation is significant.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)

items.assertion: XLP accounts for approximately 2–10% of cases with the EPP phenotype in Europe and North America.
items.reason: This is due to mutations in FECH causing increased protoporphyrin IX levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; XLP is caused by gain-of-function mutations in ALAS2.
items.reference: The Porphyrias · Congenital Erythropoietic Porphyria | ERYTHROPOIETIC PROTOPORPHYRIA (part 1)
